content.text: An economy is best understood by studying its components or sectors. Economic activities can be classified into meaningful groups based on different criteria. Classification helps us analyze how an economy functions and how its parts interact. The most common classification divides activities into primary, secondary, and tertiary sectors based on the nature of economic activity.

content.definition: A group of economic activities classified based on specific criteria. Different classification systems exist depending on what aspect of the economy we wish to understand.

content.title: Why Classify?
content.text: Classification helps us: understand how economies function, identify employment patterns, measure sectoral contributions to national output, identify development priorities, and address sector-specific problems.

notes.section_title: 2.2 Classification by Nature of Activity: Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary Sectors

content.text: The primary sector comprises activities that directly exploit natural resources. These include agriculture, dairy, fishing, forestry, and mining. In agriculture, cultivation of crops depends on natural factors like rainfall, sunshine, and climate. The output—cotton, wheat, milk—are natural products. In dairy, we depend on the biological processes of animals and availability of fodder. In mining, we extract minerals and ores from earth. The primary sector is called 'primary' because it forms the base for all other products that we make. Most natural products come from agriculture, dairy, fishing, and forestry, making this sector the foundation of all economic development.

content.title: Activities in Primary Sector
items: Agriculture: cultivation of crops dependent on natural factors (rainfall, climate, sunshine)
Dairy: production of milk and milk products based on animal biology
Fishing: extraction of fish from natural water bodies
Forestry: forest-based products (timber, herbs, etc.)
Mining: extraction of minerals and ores from earth

content.title: Why 'Primary'?
content.text: This sector is primary because it provides raw materials for other sectors. Cotton from agriculture becomes cloth in secondary sector. Sugarcane becomes sugar. These products cannot be produced by nature alone; they must be made through manufacturing.

The secondary sector comprises activities where natural products are changed into manufactured goods through various processes. These activities are associated with industries. Manufacturing is essential after the primary sector because natural products cannot be used directly; they must be processed. Using cotton fibre from the plant, we spin yarn and weave cloth. Using sugarcane, we make sugar or gur. We convert earth into bricks and use bricks to construct houses and buildings. Manufacturing can occur in factories, workshops, or at home. The secondary sector gradually became associated with different kinds of industries and is therefore also called the industrial sector.

content.title: Examples of Secondary Sector Activities
items: Textile industry: converting cotton fibre into yarn and cloth
Sugar industry: processing sugarcane into sugar
Construction industry: converting bricks and materials into buildings
Food processing: converting raw agricultural products into processed foods
Manufacturing: any process converting raw materials into finished goods

content.title: Location of Manufacturing
content.text: Secondary sector activities occur in factories (large-scale), workshops (small-scale), or even at home. Handloom weaving and pottery are examples of secondary activities occurring outside factories. The key is transformation of natural materials into manufactured goods.

The tertiary sector covers activities that help in the development of primary and secondary sectors. These activities do not produce goods themselves but provide support for production and distribution. Goods produced in primary and secondary sectors need to be transported by trucks and trains, then sold in wholesale and retail shops. They may need storage in godowns. Communication (telephone, letters) is necessary to coordinate activities. Banking provides credit for production and trade. Transport, storage, communication, banking, and trade are tertiary activities. Since these activities generate services rather than goods, the tertiary sector is also called the service sector.

content.title: Examples of Tertiary Sector Activities
items: Transport: movement of goods and people by trucks, trains, ships, planes
Storage: warehousing and godowns for produced goods
Communication: postal services, telephone services, internet
Banking: providing credit for production and trade activities
Trade: wholesale and retail shops selling goods
Essential services: teachers, doctors, lawyers, accountants, administrators
Personal services: washermen, barbers, cobblers, domestic workers
Information technology services: internet cafes, ATMs, call centres, software companies

content.title: Essential Services in Tertiary Sector
content.text: Beyond production support, tertiary sector includes essential services not directly related to producing goods: education (teachers), healthcare (doctors), law (lawyers), and administration. In recent times, information technology-based services have become important: internet cafes, ATM booths, call centres, and software companies are significant tertiary sector activities.

Although sectors are classified separately, they are highly interdependent. If farmers refuse to sell sugarcane to a sugar mill, the mill must shut down. If cotton companies stop buying from Indian markets and import from other countries, Indian cotton cultivation becomes unprofitable, and farmers may go bankrupt. If fertilizer or pumpset prices rise, farmers' cultivation costs increase and profits decline. If transporters strike and refuse to carry vegetables and milk, food becomes scarce in cities while farmers cannot sell products. This demonstrates that all sectors depend on each other for functioning.

content.title: Examples of Sectoral Interdependence
items: Primary depends on tertiary: farmers need transportation, credit, communication to sell products
Primary affects secondary: shortage of raw materials (cotton, sugarcane) affects industries
Secondary depends on tertiary: industries need transport, storage, banking for distribution
Tertiary depends on primary and secondary: services exist only because production occurs
Tertiary affects both: communication failures disrupt all sectors; credit unavailability halts production

content.title: System Perspective
content.text: Understanding sectors requires seeing them as an interconnected system. Economic activities in one sector create demand and opportunities in other sectors. Disruption in any sector affects the entire economy. Development of one sector enables development of others.

notes.section_title: 2.3 Measuring Economic Output: GDP and Gross Value Added (GVA)

content.text: With thousands of different goods and services produced—cars, computers, nails, furniture—how do we calculate total production? We cannot add quantities directly (how do you add cars to nails?). Economists use values instead of quantities. If 10,000 kg of wheat sells at Rs 20 per kg, its value is Rs 2,00,000. If 5,000 coconuts sell at Rs 15 each, their value is Rs 75,000. By calculating value of goods and services in all sectors and adding them, we get total production value.

content.title: Final Goods vs Intermediate Goods
content.text: Not all produced goods are counted. We count only final goods and services that reach consumers. Intermediate goods—used in producing final goods—should not be counted separately, as their value is already included in the final good's value. Example: wheat used to make flour, then flour used to make biscuits. Count only biscuits' value (Rs 80), which already includes wheat and flour value.

topics.topic_name: Gross Domestic Product (GDP) and Gross Value Added (GVA)

content.definition: The total value of all final goods and services produced within a country during a particular year. It shows how big the economy is and is calculated by summing production values across all sectors.
The contribution of three sectors toward national output after adjusting for taxes and subsidies. It measures sectoral contribution more accurately than GDP for policy purposes.

content.text: The task of measuring GDP is undertaken by a central government ministry with help from state and union territory governments. They collect information on total volume of goods and services and their prices, then estimate GDP. Recently, India's government began using GVA instead of sectoral GDP contributions to be consistent with global practices. GVA measures sectoral contribution after adjusting for taxes and subsidies, providing more accurate assessment of actual production.

notes.section_title: 2.4 Historical Changes and Shifting Sectoral Importance

content.text: Historical analysis of developed countries shows a clear pattern of sectoral shifts. Initially, the primary sector was most important in total production and employment. As farming methods improved and agriculture became more productive, fewer people were needed in agriculture. Crafts-persons and traders increased. Buying and selling activities expanded. With introduction of new manufacturing methods, factories expanded. Secondary sector gradually became most important in production and employment. Over the past 100 years, especially in developed countries, there has been further shift from secondary to tertiary sector. Service sector has become most important in total production, and most working people are employed in services.

content.title: Sectoral Shift Pattern in Developed Countries
items: Stage 1: Primary sector dominant (agriculture, natural resource extraction)
Stage 2: Secondary sector becomes dominant (manufacturing, industrialization)
Stage 3: Tertiary sector becomes dominant (services, information, knowledge-based activities)

content.title: Why Shifts Occur
content.text: Shifts happen because productivity improvements reduce labour needs in earlier sectors. As agriculture mechanizes, fewer farmers produce more food. People move to industries. As industries automate, people move to services. This is natural economic development, but requires managing transitions and supporting displaced workers.

notes.section_title: 2.5 Sectors in India: Production and Employment Analysis

topics.topic_name: India's Sectoral Contribution to GVA (1977-78 to 2017-18)

content.text: Over 40 years from 1977-78 to 2017-18, India's sectoral contributions changed dramatically. In 1977-78, primary sector was largest producer (about 40% of GVA), followed by secondary and tertiary. By 2017-18, this reversed: tertiary sector became largest producer (about 55% of GVA), followed by secondary and primary. Total production increased significantly—GDP grew from Rs 3,29,000 crores to Rs 12,00,000 crores. Within this growth, tertiary sector grew fastest, increasing by 14 times, while secondary grew 10 times and primary only 4 times.

content.title: Reasons for Tertiary Sector Growth
items: Basic services required: All countries need hospitals, educational institutions, postal services, police, courts, defence—services government provides
Development-driven demand: Development of agriculture and industry creates demand for transport, storage, trade, banking services
Income-driven demand: As incomes rise, people demand more services: eating out, tourism, shopping, private hospitals, private schools, professional training
Technology-driven services: Information and communication technology services—IT companies, software, call centres—have grown rapidly in the past decade

content.title: Not All Services Growing Equally
content.text: While tertiary sector grows, growth is unequal. High-skill, high-wage services (IT, professional services) grow rapidly. Low-skill services (small shopkeepers, repair persons, casual workers) struggle and barely provide living wages. Many workers in low-skill services lack alternative employment opportunities, indicating unequal development within the service sector.

A critical discrepancy exists between production and employment in India. While tertiary sector dominates production (55% of GVA), primary sector still employs most workers (about 50% or more). This is very different from developed countries, where employment shifted along with production. Why? While industrial output increased 9 times in 40 years, industrial employment increased only 3 times. Service sector production increased 14 times, but employment increased only 5 times. This means fewer workers are needed per unit of production due to mechanization and technology.

content.title: Employment-Production Gap
items: Primary sector: Employs 50% of workers but produces only 17% of GVA—farmers are underemployed
Secondary sector: Produces more than 28% of GVA but employs only 21% of workers
Tertiary sector: Produces 55% of GVA but employs only 29% of workers—most growth in this sector is in high-skill jobs
Result: More than half of India's workers are in agriculture, producing only one-sixth of national output

content.title: The Underemployment Problem
content.text: The gap between production and employment reveals underemployment, particularly in agriculture. Despite higher output, agricultural sector could release many workers without reducing production. This suggests surplus labour in agriculture seeking work elsewhere.

content.definition: Situation where people are employed but working less than what they are capable of doing, producing less than they could, or earning very low incomes from their work.

content.text: Consider Laxmi, a small farmer owning two hectares of unirrigated land dependent on rainfall. All five family members work on the plot throughout the year. Why? They have nowhere else to find work. While everyone is working, no one is fully employed—their labour is divided. Each person does some work but no one is employed at full capacity. This is underemployment: workers are apparently working but all are made to work less than their potential. This kind of underemployment is hidden compared to open unemployment (where someone has no job). Hence, it is called disguised unemployment.

content.title: Example of Disguised Unemployment in Agriculture
items: Situation: Small unirrigated farm needing 2-3 people's work can be done by 5 family members
Apparent reality: All 5 are employed on their farm
Actual reality: Productivity is divided; each person contributes less than full capacity
If 2-3 workers move to other work: Farm production remains unchanged because 2-3 were underutilized
Result: Family income increases from other work while farm production doesn't decline

content.text: Underemployment occurs not just in agriculture but also in service sector. Thousands of casual workers in urban areas search for daily employment. They work as painters, plumbers, repair persons, doing odd jobs. Many don't find work every day. Similarly, street vendors pushing carts or selling goods spend whole days but earn very little. They do this work because they lack better opportunities. This hidden underemployment affects quality of life significantly because while working, their earnings are insufficient for decent living.

content.title: Key Difference
content.text: Underemployment is different from unemployment. Unemployed person has no job. Underemployed person has a job but works below capacity or earns inadequate income. Both indicate insufficient productive employment, but unemployment is visible while underemployment is hidden in statistics.

From the underemployment analysis, three strategies emerge for increasing employment. First, expand productive capacity in agriculture through irrigation development, enabling second crops and more work days. If government constructs wells, dams, and canals, farmers can irrigate land and grow additional crops, providing employment within agriculture. Second, develop value addition and marketing. If farmers grow more crops, they need processing, storage, and transportation. Setting up dal mills, cold storages, honey collection centres creates employment beyond farming. Third, improve credit and inputs. Providing cheap agricultural credit enables farmers to buy seeds, fertilizers, equipment. This improves productivity and creates income.

content.title: Government Strategies for Employment
items: Agricultural development: Irrigation (wells, dams, canals), improved seeds, fertilizers, equipment
Value addition: Processing units, storage facilities, agricultural industries in rural areas
Infrastructure development: Roads connecting rural areas to markets, enabling product transportation
Human capital: Education and healthcare job creation (20 lakh jobs potential in education sector alone, 35 lakh in tourism)
Credit provision: Banks providing cheap credit at reasonable interest rates to farmers and small entrepreneurs

topics.topic_name: Mahatma Gandhi National Employment Guarantee Act (MGNREGA) 2005

content.definition: A central government law guaranteeing 100 days of employment per year to all those able to work and in need of work in rural areas. It is referred to as 'Right to Work' legislation.

content.text: Recognizing that long-term employment strategies require time to implement, the government created MGNREGA 2005, implementing the Right to Work. Under this Act, all those able and in need of work in rural areas are guaranteed 100 days of employment in a year by the government. If government fails to provide employment, it must pay unemployment allowances. The types of work undertaken should help increase production from land (ponds, wells, roads in rural areas). MGNREGA operates in about 625 districts of India, providing crucial short-term income support while longer-term employment strategies develop.

notes.section_title: 2.8 Classification by Employment Conditions: Organised and Unorganised Sectors

content.text: The organised sector covers enterprises or places of work where employment terms are regular, clearly defined, and regulated by government rules. Workers are registered with the government and employers must follow rules and regulations given in various laws such as the Factories Act, Minimum Wages Act, Payment of Gratuity Act, Shops and Establishments Act. Workers in organised sector have job security and are expected to work fixed hours. If they work more, they receive overtime payment.

content.title: Benefits in Organised Sector
items: Job security: Regular employment with written appointment letters
Fixed working hours: Defined hours with overtime compensation if exceeded
Leave benefits: Paid leave, holidays, sick leave as per government rules
Financial benefits: Provident fund, gratuity, medical allowances
Work environment: Safe working conditions, drinking water, facilities as per law
Retirement benefits: Pensions upon retirement for many organised sector workers

content.title: Who Works in Organised Sector?
content.text: Employees in government offices, large factories, banks, schools, and registered private companies work in organised sector. Some self-employed persons (doctors, lawyers with registered offices) also fall in this category.

The unorganised sector comprises small and scattered units largely outside government control. Though rules and regulations exist, they are often not followed. Jobs here are low-paid, often not regular, with no job security. Employment can be terminated without reason. Wages are paid only for days worked—no paid leave or holidays. Workers receive no overtime, medical benefits, or retirement pensions. This sector includes landless agricultural labourers, small farmers, street vendors, repair persons, domestic workers, handloom weavers, and casual workers in construction, trade, and transport.

content.title: Characteristics of Unorganised Sector
items: Low and irregular wages: Pay depends on work availability and employer's whims
No job security: Can be asked to leave without reason
No benefits: No leave, holidays, medical, or retirement benefits
Poor working conditions: Often unsafe and unhygienic working environments
Vulnerable workers: Landless labourers, artisans, casual workers, street vendors
Self-employed struggling: Small farmers, small traders with minimal income

content.title: Vulnerability in Unorganised Sector
content.text: Workers in unorganised sector lack protection and often face exploitation. Vulnerable groups—scheduled castes, tribes, backward communities—are overrepresented in unorganised sector work, adding social discrimination to economic hardship.

In rural areas, unorganised sector comprises landless agricultural labourers, small and marginal farmers, sharecroppers, and artisans (weavers, blacksmiths, carpenters, goldsmiths). Nearly 80% of rural households are small and marginal farmers. In urban areas, it includes workers in small-scale industries, casual workers in construction, trade, transport, street vendors, head load workers, garment makers, and rag pickers. Small-scale industries need government support for raw materials and marketing. Casual workers require employment protection and income security.

content.title: Rural and Urban Vulnerable Groups
items: Rural vulnerable: Landless labourers, small/marginal farmers, sharecroppers, artisans (80% of rural households)
Urban vulnerable: Casual construction workers, street vendors, head load workers, garment makers, rag pickers
Cross-cutting vulnerability: Workers from scheduled castes, tribes, backward communities face both economic and social discrimination
All unorganised workers: Lack employment security, social protection, and fair wages

content.text: Protection and support for unorganised sector workers is necessary for both economic and social development. Rural farmers need timely supply of seeds, agricultural inputs, credit, storage facilities, and marketing outlets. Small-scale industries need procurement support and market access. Casual workers in both rural and urban areas need employment protection—minimum wages, written agreements, safe working conditions. Government should provide credit at reasonable interest rates, ensure price supports for agricultural products, and regulate working conditions even in unorganised sector. Social protection—health insurance, disability coverage—is essential for workers lacking regular incomes.

content.title: Protection Framework Needed
items: Economic support: Credit provision, price supports, market access for farmers and small businesses
Employment protection: Minimum wages, written agreements, safe working conditions for casual workers
Social support: Health insurance, disability coverage, pension schemes for workers without regular income
Inclusive approach: Special protection for backward communities and groups facing discrimination
Capacity building: Skills training and education enabling workers to transition to better-paid work

notes.section_title: 2.10 Classification by Ownership: Public and Private Sectors

content.definition: Sector where the government owns most assets and provides services. Purpose is not just earning profits but meeting public needs and welfare.
Sector where ownership of assets and service delivery is in hands of private individuals or companies. Primary motive is earning profits.

content.text: Railways and post offices are examples of public sector enterprises owned and operated by government. Tata Iron and Steel Company Limited (TISCO) and Reliance Industries Limited are privately owned companies. Activities in private sector are guided by profit motive. However, some services required by society as a whole but unprofitable for private sector must be provided by government. Government raises money through taxes to cover costs.

Several things needed by society cannot be provided by private sector at reasonable cost. Large-scale infrastructure like roads, bridges, railways, harbours requires enormous investment beyond private capacity. Generating and supplying electricity nationwide requires government coordination. Providing irrigation through large dams benefits entire regions. Private individuals cannot afford these massive costs. Also, profitability may prevent private sector from providing certain services. Private sector will not establish schools or hospitals in remote areas where few can afford fees. Government education and healthcare ensure universal access. Similarly, government intervenes in agriculture, buying wheat and rice at fair prices and distributing through ration shops, protecting both farmers and consumers.

content.title: Services Government Must Provide
items: Infrastructure: Roads, bridges, railways, harbours, dams—too expensive for private sector
Utilities: Electricity generation and distribution at affordable rates
Education: Public schools making education accessible to poor families, increasing overall literacy
Healthcare: Public hospitals and health services for all, not just wealthy
Food security: Buying agricultural products from farmers at fair prices, distributing through ration shops
Safety and security: Police, defence, emergency services protecting all citizens
Social protection: Pensions, unemployment benefits, disability coverage for vulnerable groups

content.title: Government Cost-Sharing
content.text: In many services like electricity provision or agriculture support, government bears part of cost, making services affordable for common people. Public provision ensures equity—services available to all regardless of income—something private market cannot guarantee.

glossary.definition: Economic activities directly exploiting natural resources such as agriculture, dairy, fishing, forestry, and mining. Forms the base for all economic activity.
Economic activities involving transformation of natural products into manufactured goods through processing and manufacturing. Also called industrial sector.

Economic activities providing support and services for production and distribution of goods. Includes transport, trade, banking, education, healthcare, and information technology services. Also called service sector.
Total value of all final goods and services produced within a country during a particular year. Indicates the size of the economy.
Contribution of three sectors toward national output after adjusting for taxes and subsidies. More accurately measures sectoral contribution than sectoral GDP.
Goods and services that directly reach consumers for use or consumption. Their value includes value of all intermediate goods used in production.
Goods used in producing final goods and services. Their value should not be counted separately as it is already included in the final good's value.
Situation where employed persons work less than what they are capable of doing or earn very low incomes, producing less than potential. Often hidden in statistics.
Hidden unemployment where workers appear employed but all work below capacity. Common in agriculture where family members share limited work.
Sector with regular employment, clearly defined terms, government registration, and regulated working conditions. Provides job security and various benefits.
Sector with small scattered units, irregular employment, low wages, no job security, and lack of benefits. Regulations are often not followed.
Sector where government owns assets and provides services. Purpose extends beyond profits to meeting public needs and welfare.
Sector where private individuals or companies own assets and provide services primarily for profit.
Mahatma Gandhi National Employment Guarantee Act guaranteeing 100 days of employment annually to those able and in need of work in rural areas. Known as 'Right to Work' legislation.
Concept that sectors depend on each other for functioning. Disruption in one sector affects others. All sectors must develop together.
Process of creating more valuable products from raw materials. Example: converting agricultural products into processed foods increases value.

summary_points: Economic activities are classified into three sectors by nature: primary (agriculture, mining), secondary (manufacturing, industries), and tertiary (services). These sectors are interdependent—disruption in any sector affects others. Development requires balanced growth across sectors.

India has experienced dramatic sectoral shifts: primary sector's contribution fell from 40% (1977-78) to 17% (2017-18) of GVA, while tertiary sector rose to 55%. However, employment patterns did not shift similarly—over 50% of workers still depend on agriculture, indicating severe underemployment.

Underemployment, particularly disguised unemployment in agriculture, is a critical challenge. Many workers are employed but produce less than capacity. If small farmers' workers moved to other employment, farm production would remain unchanged while family income increased, showing agriculture has surplus labour.

The organised sector (government offices, large factories) provides job security, benefits, and protection through laws. The unorganised sector (80% of workers) offers low-pay, irregular work with no security or benefits. Most workers—especially vulnerable groups—work in unorganised sector facing exploitation.

Government must provide certain services (education, healthcare, infrastructure, utilities) that private sector cannot profitably provide. Public sector ensures universal access regardless of income. MGNREGA 2005 guarantees 100 days of rural employment annually, addressing short-term underemployment while long-term strategies develop.

Protecting unorganised sector workers requires credit provision, fair prices for agricultural products, employment protection (minimum wages, safe conditions), and social protection (health insurance, pensions). Particularly, landless labourers, small farmers, and vulnerable communities need targeted support for economic and social development.
